item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Discerning Structural Influences on Stereoselectivity and Kinetics in Glycosylations With Oxathiane Donors
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
An NMR based study of glycosylations using oxathiane‐type glycosyl donors is presented. The impact of structural variation on reaction kinetics and stereochemical outcome is assessed, including protecting group derivation and auxiliary structure. Methods of tuning glycosylation kinetics without compromising stereoselectivity are demonstrated, while also showing structural combinations that render this strategy ineffective.
Trans‐decalin‐like sulfonium oxathiane glycosyl donors have been previously shown as a viable route to access 1,2‐cis‐glycosides, though these reports have indicated that donor structure has a large influence on glycosylation outcome. This work describes the synthesis of an array of methyl oxathiane donors and their subsequent use in discerning the effect of structural differences on stereoselectivity and reactivity in glycosylations. In particular, introduction of 3‐methyl groups in the oxathiane scaffold allowed kinetic tuning of the glycosyl donor without diminishing α‐selectivity.
item.content:encoded:
An NMR based study of glycosylations using oxathiane-type glycosyl donors is presented. The impact of structural variation on reaction kinetics and stereochemical outcome is assessed, including protecting group derivation and auxiliary structure. Methods of tuning glycosylation kinetics without compromising stereoselectivity are demonstrated, while also showing structural combinations that render this strategy ineffective.


Trans-decalin-like sulfonium oxathiane glycosyl donors have been previously shown as a viable route to access 1,2-cis-glycosides, though these reports have indicated that donor structure has a large influence on glycosylation outcome. This work describes the synthesis of an array of methyl oxathiane donors and their subsequent use in discerning the effect of structural differences on stereoselectivity and reactivity in glycosylations. In particular, introduction of 3-methyl groups in the oxathiane scaffold allowed kinetic tuning of the glycosyl donor without diminishing α-selectivity.
item.dc:creator: Matthew E. Warnes, Martin A. Fascione
item.dc:title: Discerning Structural Influences on Stereoselectivity and Kinetics in Glycosylations With Oxathiane Donors

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Lipase–V‐MPS Co‐Catalyzed Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Tertiary Alcohols: Process Intensification Through Catalyst Compartmentalization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Dynamic kinetic resolution of tert‐alcohols co‐catalyzed by lipase and V‐MPS4 is reported, enabled by compartmentalization using a polydimethylsiloxane thimble. Physical separation of V‐MPS4 from lipase and Na2CO3 was crucial for preserving its racemization activity. This method has significantly shortened reaction time, expanded substrate scope, and represented a streamlined procedure.
Chemoenzymatic dynamic kinetic resolution (DKR) of racemic alcohols represents a straightforward and sustainable strategy for synthesizing enantiomerically pure alcohols and their derivatives, which serve as important synthetic intermediates in the fine chemical industry, yet its application to tert‐alcohols remains challenging due to steric hindrance of the alcohols and inefficient racemization methods. This study presents significant advancement in the chemoenzymatic DKR of tert‐alcohols by a compartmentalization strategy using a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) thimble, which physically separates involved two reactions, i.e., the lipase‐catalyzed enantioselective esterification and racemization, in a single flask. Using commercially available immobilized lipase A from Candida antarctica (CAL‐A) and our original, vanadium‐based racemization catalyst V‐MPS4, we thoroughly optimized the reaction conditions and found that the use of excess Na2CO3 and prewashing of CAL‐A were effective in ensuring a high stability of V‐MPS4. With additional adjustments of some factors, such as the amount of V‐MPS4, applying our protocol led to the synthesis of enantiomerically enriched esters with up to 99% enantiomeric excess (ee) from seven tert‐alcohols, in 52%–62% isolated yields in 48 h. These results demonstrate significant improvements in reaction time, catalyst stability, substrate scope, and reaction procedure, highlighting the practical utility of compartmentalized chemoenzymatic DKR for challenging tert‐alcohol substrates.
item.content:encoded:
Dynamic kinetic resolution of tert-alcohols co-catalyzed by lipase and V-MPS4 is reported, enabled by compartmentalization using a polydimethylsiloxane thimble. Physical separation of V-MPS4 from lipase and Na2CO3 was crucial for preserving its racemization activity. This method has significantly shortened reaction time, expanded substrate scope, and represented a streamlined procedure.


Chemoenzymatic dynamic kinetic resolution (DKR) of racemic alcohols represents a straightforward and sustainable strategy for synthesizing enantiomerically pure alcohols and their derivatives, which serve as important synthetic intermediates in the fine chemical industry, yet its application to tert-alcohols remains challenging due to steric hindrance of the alcohols and inefficient racemization methods. This study presents significant advancement in the chemoenzymatic DKR of tert-alcohols by a compartmentalization strategy using a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) thimble, which physically separates involved two reactions, i.e., the lipase-catalyzed enantioselective esterification and racemization, in a single flask. Using commercially available immobilized lipase A from Candida antarctica (CAL-A) and our original, vanadium-based racemization catalyst V-MPS4, we thoroughly optimized the reaction conditions and found that the use of excess Na2CO3 and prewashing of CAL-A were effective in ensuring a high stability of V-MPS4. With additional adjustments of some factors, such as the amount of V-MPS4, applying our protocol led to the synthesis of enantiomerically enriched esters with up to 99% enantiomeric excess (ee) from seven tert-alcohols, in 52%–62% isolated yields in 48 h. These results demonstrate significant improvements in reaction time, catalyst stability, substrate scope, and reaction procedure, highlighting the practical utility of compartmentalized chemoenzymatic DKR for challenging tert-alcohol substrates.
item.dc:creator: Satoshi Horino, Karla Wagner, Anke Hummel, Kyohei Kanomata, Harald Gröger, Shuji Akai
item.dc:title: Lipase–V‐MPS Co‐Catalyzed Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of Tertiary Alcohols: Process Intensification Through Catalyst Compartmentalization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Zirconium Oxo Cluster‐Catalyzed Dipeptide Cyclization Evaluated by Experiments and Theory
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Direct intramolecular peptide bondformation catalysed by Zr oxo cluster catalysts leading to an one‐stepformation of 2,5‐diketopiperazines from readily available dipeptides is investigatedthrough experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
Although peptides are essential for many areas of chemistry, key limitations still remain in their synthesis, such as high costs and poor atom economy. An ideal alternative is the catalytic peptide bond formation directly from nonactivated amino acids; however, the high‐energy barrier associated with this reaction hampers the development of suitable alternatives. In this work, we evaluated the catalytic activity of discrete zirconium oxo clusters for direct peptide bond formation, using dipeptide cyclization as model reaction. The clusters afforded several 2,5‐diketopiperazine derivatives in good‐to‐excellent yields under straightforward open‐flask conditions, without requiring the water byproduct to be scavenged from the reaction. Further mechanistic study through density functional theory calculations revealed that the mechanism involves a second substrate molecule near the reactive site of the catalysts, to streamline proton transfers that push the reaction forward. These results underline the promising potential of discrete ZrOCs as an emerging class of catalysts for the formation of peptide bonds under green, straightforward reaction conditions.
item.content:encoded:
Direct intramolecular peptide bondformation catalysed by Zr oxo cluster catalysts leading to an one-stepformation of 2,5-diketopiperazines from readily available dipeptides is investigatedthrough experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.


Although peptides are essential for many areas of chemistry, key limitations still remain in their synthesis, such as high costs and poor atom economy. An ideal alternative is the catalytic peptide bond formation directly from nonactivated amino acids; however, the high-energy barrier associated with this reaction hampers the development of suitable alternatives. In this work, we evaluated the catalytic activity of discrete zirconium oxo clusters for direct peptide bond formation, using dipeptide cyclization as model reaction. The clusters afforded several 2,5-diketopiperazine derivatives in good-to-excellent yields under straightforward open-flask conditions, without requiring the water byproduct to be scavenged from the reaction. Further mechanistic study through density functional theory calculations revealed that the mechanism involves a second substrate molecule near the reactive site of the catalysts, to streamline proton transfers that push the reaction forward. These results underline the promising potential of discrete ZrOCs as an emerging class of catalysts for the formation of peptide bonds under green, straightforward reaction conditions.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Peeters, Jordi Puiggalí‐Jou, Yujie Zhang, Ismail Y. Kokculer, Albert Solé‐Daura, Jorge J. Carbó, Tatjana N. Parac‐Vogt, Francisco de Azambuja
item.dc:title: Zirconium Oxo Cluster‐Catalyzed Dipeptide Cyclization Evaluated by Experiments and Theory

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Triptindane‐9,10,11‐triol: Threefold Hydride Transfer from Lithium Aluminum Hydride as the Key Step to C3‐Symmetrically Trifunctionalized Tribenzo[3.3.3]propellanes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Threefold reduction of 9,10,11‐triptindanetrione with LiAlH4 or NaBH4 yields exclusively the C3‐symmetric triol, suggesting an intracomplex (“rolling”) mechanism. However, use of LiAlH4/LiAlD4 (1 : 1) reveals a preceding hydride exchange. Mechanistic implications are discussed including the reduction of the related diketone. Mostly C3‐stereospecific conversion of the triol to further C3‐trifunctionalized congeners via the tribromide is presented.
The C3v‐symmetric framework of triptindane (C3v‐tribenzo[3.3.3]propellane, 1) was equipped with three benzylic monovalent functional groups in a C3‐symmetric orientation. Key reaction was the reduction of 9,10,11‐triptindanetrione (2) with lithium aluminum hydride or sodium borohydride giving the corresponding C3‐symmetric triol 5 in high yield and perfect diastereoselectivity. The diastereoselective reduction of 9,10‐triptindanedione 9 with LiAlH4 was also studied for comparison. Conversion of triol 5 to the respective tribromide 13, trichloride 14, and triiodide 15 and subsequent refunctionalization of 13 to 9,10,11‐triaminotripindane 18 via the corresponding triazide 16 was achieved with preservation of C3‐symmetry in each case. In contrast, the C1‐triazide 17 is formed along with 16 under Lewis‐acid conditions, enabling an access to the C1‐symmetric triamine 19. The increased torsion about the propellane axis of tribromotriptindane 13 was determined by X‐ray crystallography. There is strong evidence that the reduction 2 → 5 occurs by fast intracomplex transfer of three hydrides from the same alanate ion. In turn, attempts to confirm this by use of an equimolar mixture of LiAlH4 and LiAlD4 were nonsupportive; rather, they point to a hitherto unknown fast hydride exchange occurring in etheral alanate solutions.
item.content:encoded:
Threefold reduction of 9,10,11-triptindanetrione with LiAlH4 or NaBH4 yields exclusively the C 3-symmetric triol, suggesting an intracomplex (“rolling”) mechanism. However, use of LiAlH4/LiAlD4 (1 : 1) reveals a preceding hydride exchange. Mechanistic implications are discussed including the reduction of the related diketone. Mostly C 3-stereospecific conversion of the triol to further C 3-trifunctionalized congeners via the tribromide is presented.


The C 3v -symmetric framework of triptindane (C 3v -tribenzo[3.3.3]propellane, 1) was equipped with three benzylic monovalent functional groups in a C 3-symmetric orientation. Key reaction was the reduction of 9,10,11-triptindanetrione (2) with lithium aluminum hydride or sodium borohydride giving the corresponding C 3-symmetric triol 5 in high yield and perfect diastereoselectivity. The diastereoselective reduction of 9,10-triptindanedione 9 with LiAlH4 was also studied for comparison. Conversion of triol 5 to the respective tribromide 13, trichloride 14, and triiodide 15 and subsequent refunctionalization of 13 to 9,10,11-triaminotripindane 18 via the corresponding triazide 16 was achieved with preservation of C 3-symmetry in each case. In contrast, the C 1-triazide 17 is formed along with 16 under Lewis-acid conditions, enabling an access to the C 1-symmetric triamine 19. The increased torsion about the propellane axis of tribromotriptindane 13 was determined by X-ray crystallography. There is strong evidence that the reduction 2 → 5 occurs by fast intracomplex transfer of three hydrides from the same alanate ion. In turn, attempts to confirm this by use of an equimolar mixture of LiAlH4 and LiAlD4 were nonsupportive; rather, they point to a hitherto unknown fast hydride exchange occurring in etheral alanate solutions.
item.dc:creator: Thorsten Hackfort, Bernhard M. Malorny, Andreas Mix, Beate Neumann, Hans‐Georg Stammler, Dietmar Kuck
item.dc:title: Triptindane‐9,10,11‐triol: Threefold Hydride Transfer from Lithium Aluminum Hydride as the Key Step to C3‐Symmetrically Trifunctionalized Tribenzo[3.3.3]propellanes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Synthesis of C1–C18 Fragment with Macrocyclic Framework of Marine Cytotoxic (‐)‐Callyspongiolide
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
A convergent approach for the synthesis of C1–C18 fragment of (−)‐Callyspongiolide macrocylic framework is presented .
A stereoselective convergent strategy for the synthesis of the C1–C18 fragment of callyspongiolide featuring a protected 14‐membered macrocyclic core is reported. This approach highlights the viability of constructing complex macrolide framework through stereoselective methods such as asymmetric alkylation and the Evans aldol reaction enabling high level of stereocontrol and sets the stage for the total synthesis of callyspongiolide. The synthesis also involves the coupling of two key fragments—an acid and an alcohol, both bearing terminal olefins—via an esterification reaction, followed by a ring‐closing metathesis (RCM) reaction as the key reactions to construct the macrocycle core.
item.content:encoded:
A convergent approach for the synthesis of C1–C18 fragment of (−)-Callyspongiolide macrocylic framework is presented .


A stereoselective convergent strategy for the synthesis of the C1–C18 fragment of callyspongiolide featuring a protected 14-membered macrocyclic core is reported. This approach highlights the viability of constructing complex macrolide framework through stereoselective methods such as asymmetric alkylation and the Evans aldol reaction enabling high level of stereocontrol and sets the stage for the total synthesis of callyspongiolide. The synthesis also involves the coupling of two key fragments—an acid and an alcohol, both bearing terminal olefins—via an esterification reaction, followed by a ring-closing metathesis (RCM) reaction as the key reactions to construct the macrocycle core.
item.dc:creator: Sankara Rao Patta, Naresh Gantasala, Pradeep P. Desale, Srihari Pabbaraja
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Synthesis of C1–C18 Fragment with Macrocyclic Framework of Marine Cytotoxic (‐)‐Callyspongiolide

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sequential Ag(I)/Pd(0) Relay Catalysis Toward (4+3) Oxadiazepine Cycloadducts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description: Herein, we report a sequential Ag(I)/Pd(0) relay catalysis strategy enabling (4 + 3) cycloadditions between in situ generated C, N‐azomethine imines and 2‐methylidenetrimethylene carbonates. After opti mization of the reaction conditions, an array of isoquinoline‐fused oxadiazepines was obtained showing broad functional group tolerance. The method was further demonstrated on gram scale and through a streamlined three‐step/one‐pot procedure from readily available ortho‐alkynylbenzaldehydes. This work demonstrates that orthogonal Ag(I)/Pd(0) relay catalysis provides a powerful and general platform for the rapid construction of complex heterocyclic scaffolds.
item.content:encoded: Herein, we report a sequential Ag(I)/Pd(0) relay catalysis strategy enabling (4 + 3) cycloadditions between in situ generated C, N-azomethine imines and 2-methylidenetrimethylene carbonates. After opti mization of the reaction conditions, an array of isoquinoline-fused oxadiazepines was obtained showing broad functional group tolerance. The method was further demonstrated on gram scale and through a streamlined three-step/one-pot procedure from readily available ortho-alkynylbenzaldehydes. This work demonstrates that orthogonal Ag(I)/Pd(0) relay catalysis provides a powerful and general platform for the rapid construction of complex heterocyclic scaffolds.
item.dc:creator: Antoine Roblin, Adrien Tintar, Nicolas Casaretto, Alexis Archambeau
item.dc:title: Sequential Ag(I)/Pd(0) Relay Catalysis Toward (4+3) Oxadiazepine Cycloadducts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed Sequential Azide‐Alkyne Cycloaddition (CuAAc) and Ullmann Coupling: A Tandem One‐Pot Synthesis of Quinazolino[3,2‐c][1,2,3] Triazolo[1,5‐a]‐Quinazolin‐14‐One
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed tandem multicomponent strategy has been successfully established as an efficient approach for the synthesis of triazole‐fused polycyclic quinazolino[3,2‐c][1,2,3]triazolo[1,5‐a]quinazolin‐14‐ones from 2‐bromophenyl quinazolinones, sodium azide, and terminal alkynes. The methodology integrates CuAAC, Ullmann‐type C—N coupling and intramolecular C—N bond formation under ligand‐free conditions, affording structurally complex polycyclic products in good yields with broad substrate scope.
An efficient copper‐catalyzed tandem strategy for the construction of triazole‐fused polycyclic quinazolinone frameworks has been developed. This methodology is accomplished through a multicomponent protocol involving 2‐bromophenyl quinazolinones, sodium azide, and terminal alkynes and proceeds via copper‐catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC), Ullmann‐type C—N coupling, and intramolecular C—N bond formation, delivering quinazolino[3,2‐c] [1,2,3] triazolo[1,5‐a] quinazolin‐14‐ones in 60%–78% yields. Current protocol provides a concise and valuable route for constructing structurally complex polycyclic N‐heterocycles with potential pharmaceutical relevance.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-catalyzed tandem multicomponent strategy has been successfully established as an efficient approach for the synthesis of triazole-fused polycyclic quinazolino[3,2-c][1,2,3]triazolo[1,5-a]quinazolin-14-ones from 2-bromophenyl quinazolinones, sodium azide, and terminal alkynes. The methodology integrates CuAAC, Ullmann-type C—N coupling and intramolecular C—N bond formation under ligand-free conditions, affording structurally complex polycyclic products in good yields with broad substrate scope.


An efficient copper-catalyzed tandem strategy for the construction of triazole-fused polycyclic quinazolinone frameworks has been developed. This methodology is accomplished through a multicomponent protocol involving 2-bromophenyl quinazolinones, sodium azide, and terminal alkynes and proceeds via copper-catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC), Ullmann-type C—N coupling, and intramolecular C—N bond formation, delivering quinazolino[3,2-c] [1,2,3] triazolo[1,5-a] quinazolin-14-ones in 60%–78% yields. Current protocol provides a concise and valuable route for constructing structurally complex polycyclic N-heterocycles with potential pharmaceutical relevance.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Kandula, Gal Reddy Potuganti, Shreya Patlolla, Manjula Alla
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed Sequential Azide‐Alkyne Cycloaddition (CuAAc) and Ullmann Coupling: A Tandem One‐Pot Synthesis of Quinazolino[3,2‐c][1,2,3] Triazolo[1,5‐a]‐Quinazolin‐14‐One

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed Asymmetric Michael Reaction of α‐Substituted Cyanoacetates with Acrylates and Acrylamides with Prolinol‐Phosphine‐sec‐Amine Chiral Ligand
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed enantioselective Michael reaction of α‐substituted cyanoacetates with acrylates was developed as the first highly enantioselective method for this substrate class, enabling construction of quaternary stereogenic centers in acyclic systems. A Cu–prolinol–phosphine–sec‐amine ligand system (L1) ensured high reactivity and enantioselectivity, tolerating various α‐substituents. The catalysis also applied to acrylamides, and selective transformations of ester and nitrile groups were demonstrated.
Copper‐catalyzed enantioselective Michael reaction of α‐substituted cyanoacetates with acrylates was developed as the first highly enantioselective cyanoacetate Michael reaction with acrylate‐class substrates, providing a new method for constructing quaternary stereogenic carbon centers in acyclic systems. The reaction was efficiently promoted by a Cu‐prolinol‐phosphine‐sec‐amine chiral ligand L1 system, achieving both high reactivity and enantioselectivity. The α‐substituents of the cyanoacetates were not restricted to a methyl group; ethyl, butyl, isobutyl, and others were also tolerated. Moreover, the catalysis was applicable to reactions with acrylamides, which had not been employed in asymmetric cyanoester Michael reaction. Selective transformations of both ester and nitrile groups in the multifunctional Michael addition products were demonstrated.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-catalyzed enantioselective Michael reaction of α-substituted cyanoacetates with acrylates was developed as the first highly enantioselective method for this substrate class, enabling construction of quaternary stereogenic centers in acyclic systems. A Cu–prolinol–phosphine–sec-amine ligand system (L1) ensured high reactivity and enantioselectivity, tolerating various α-substituents. The catalysis also applied to acrylamides, and selective transformations of ester and nitrile groups were demonstrated.


Copper-catalyzed enantioselective Michael reaction of α-substituted cyanoacetates with acrylates was developed as the first highly enantioselective cyanoacetate Michael reaction with acrylate-class substrates, providing a new method for constructing quaternary stereogenic carbon centers in acyclic systems. The reaction was efficiently promoted by a Cu-prolinol-phosphine-sec-amine chiral ligand L1 system, achieving both high reactivity and enantioselectivity. The α-substituents of the cyanoacetates were not restricted to a methyl group; ethyl, butyl, isobutyl, and others were also tolerated. Moreover, the catalysis was applicable to reactions with acrylamides, which had not been employed in asymmetric cyanoester Michael reaction. Selective transformations of both ester and nitrile groups in the multifunctional Michael addition products were demonstrated.
item.dc:creator: Mai Onizawa, Satoshi Sakai, Irtaza Qureshi, Yohei Shimizu, Masaya Sawamura
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed Asymmetric Michael Reaction of α‐Substituted Cyanoacetates with Acrylates and Acrylamides with Prolinol‐Phosphine‐sec‐Amine Chiral Ligand

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Organophotoredox‐Catalyzed [3 + 2] Cycloaddition for the Diastereoselective Synthesis of Spirocyclopentane Oxindoles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
A novel way to access spirocyclopentane oxindoles was developed based on an organophotoredox‐catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition reaction between N‐aryl cyclopropylamines and isatin‐derived alkenes.
A novel organophotoredox‐catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition strategy for the synthesis of spirocyclopentane oxindoles is reported, employing N‐aryl cyclopropylamines and isatin‐derived alkenes as readily available coupling partners. This protocol affords diverse spirocyclopentane oxindole derivatives in excellent yields (up to 94%) and good diastereoselectivities (up to 96:4 dr) under mild conditions. The transformation is facilitated by a dual catalytic system consisting of Eosin Y as photoredox catalyst and diphenyl phosphate (PA) as organocatalyst, with N,N‐diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) as a crucial additive.
item.content:encoded:
A novel way to access spirocyclopentane oxindoles was developed based on an organophotoredox-catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition reaction between N-aryl cyclopropylamines and isatin-derived alkenes.


A novel organophotoredox-catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition strategy for the synthesis of spirocyclopentane oxindoles is reported, employing N-aryl cyclopropylamines and isatin-derived alkenes as readily available coupling partners. This protocol affords diverse spirocyclopentane oxindole derivatives in excellent yields (up to 94%) and good diastereoselectivities (up to 96:4 dr) under mild conditions. The transformation is facilitated by a dual catalytic system consisting of Eosin Y as photoredox catalyst and diphenyl phosphate (PA) as organocatalyst, with N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) as a crucial additive.
item.dc:creator: Yingjie Huang, Xufeng Lin
item.dc:title: Organophotoredox‐Catalyzed [3 + 2] Cycloaddition for the Diastereoselective Synthesis of Spirocyclopentane Oxindoles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of a Carbon‐Linked Acyldepsipeptide Derivative
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
The synthesis of a methylene‐linked acyldepsipeptide (ADEP) is reported. Success relied upon an improve method to generate a versatile vinyl glycine methyl ester synthon and a rationally optimized Grubb’s metathesis. NMR and in silico studies revealed an unusual trans–trans proline orientation for this new unnatural ADEP analog, advancing the understanding of ADEP structure–activity relationships and potentially cyclic peptidomimetics in general.
We report the synthesis and conformational analysis of a methylene‐linked acyldepsipeptide (ADEP). The convergent approach hinged on the generation of a protected vinylglycine synthon, a versatile noncanonical amino acid building block. A rationally optimized Grubbs metathesis and a new mode for macrocyclization to provide critical intermediates are also featured. NMR and in silico studies revealed an unusual trans–trans proline orientation for this new unnatural analog, advancing the understanding of ADEP structure–activity relationships and cyclic peptidomimetics in general.
item.content:encoded:
The synthesis of a methylene-linked acyldepsipeptide (ADEP) is reported. Success relied upon an improve method to generate a versatile vinyl glycine methyl ester synthon and a rationally optimized Grubb’s metathesis. NMR and in silico studies revealed an unusual trans –trans proline orientation for this new unnatural ADEP analog, advancing the understanding of ADEP structure–activity relationships and potentially cyclic peptidomimetics in general.


We report the synthesis and conformational analysis of a methylene-linked acyldepsipeptide (ADEP). The convergent approach hinged on the generation of a protected vinylglycine synthon, a versatile noncanonical amino acid building block. A rationally optimized Grubbs metathesis and a new mode for macrocyclization to provide critical intermediates are also featured. NMR and in silico studies revealed an unusual trans–trans proline orientation for this new unnatural analog, advancing the understanding of ADEP structure–activity relationships and cyclic peptidomimetics in general.

item.title: Front Cover: Copper‐Catalyzed Sequential Azide‐Alkyne Cycloaddition (CuAAc) and Ullmann Coupling: A Tandem One‐Pot Synthesis of Quinazolino[3,2‐c][1,2,3] Triazolo[1,5‐a]‐Quinazolin‐14‐One (Eur. J. Org. Chem. 15/2026)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description: The Front Cover shows a copper‐catalyzed tandem strategy that enables the efficient synthesis of triazole‐fused quinazolinones through a multicomponent reaction of 2‐bromophenyl quinazolinones, sodium azide, and terminal alkynes. Integrating CuAAC and Ulmann‐type C–N coupling under mild, ligand‐free conditions, this method yields diverse polycyclic N‐heterocycles with broad substrate scope. More information can be found in the Research Article by M. Alla and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.202501064).

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Total Synthesis of Polyacetylene‐Based Natural Products Facilitated by Cadiot–Chodkiewicz Coupling Reaction: A Review
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
Cadiot–Chodkiewicz cross‐coupling reaction is a pivotal organic transformation employed for the synthesis of polyacetylenes or conjugated enynes from the reaction of haloalkynes and terminal alkynes. This reaction has gained significance prominence in designing the intricate biological active natural products and other synthetic molecules comprising polyacetylene functionality.
Copper‐mediated carbon–carbon bond formation reactions are significant transformations in organic synthesis. In this context, Cadiot–Chodkiewicz cross‐coupling reaction has played a pivotal role in the designing of natural products, offering an efficient and powerful way to build intricate polyacetylenes. This account provides recent applications of Cadiot–Chodkiewicz coupling reaction toward the total synthesis of polyacetylene‐based natural products.
item.content:encoded:
Cadiot–Chodkiewicz cross-coupling reaction is a pivotal organic transformation employed for the synthesis of polyacetylenes or conjugated enynes from the reaction of haloalkynes and terminal alkynes. This reaction has gained significance prominence in designing the intricate biological active natural products and other synthetic molecules comprising polyacetylene functionality.


Copper-mediated carbon–carbon bond formation reactions are significant transformations in organic synthesis. In this context, Cadiot–Chodkiewicz cross-coupling reaction has played a pivotal role in the designing of natural products, offering an efficient and powerful way to build intricate polyacetylenes. This account provides recent applications of Cadiot–Chodkiewicz coupling reaction toward the total synthesis of polyacetylene-based natural products.

item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of Polyacetylene‐Based Natural Products Facilitated by Cadiot–Chodkiewicz Coupling Reaction: A Review

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Tetraamino‐Benzoquinone‐Based Organic Materials for Advanced Energy Conversion and Storage
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
The tetraamino‐benzoquinone (TABQ) unit is a promising building block to construct high‐performance electrode materials for rechargeable batteries due to its cost‐effectiveness, eco‐friendliness, and functional versatility.
Quinone compounds have been widely demonstrated as highly promising electrode materials for rechargeable batteries due to their cost‐effectiveness, eco‐friendliness, and multifunctionality. Among them, tetraamino‐benzoquinone (TABQ) has garnered significant attention as a common building block owing to its potential for large‐scale production and unique molecular design flexibility. The amino (–NH2) group in TABQ not only confers its multifunctionality in batteries but also enables the construction of one‐ to three‐dimensional electrode materials. These structural advantages give TABQ‐based electrode materials significant application potential in various rechargeable batteries. This minireview first outlines the design strategies and operating principles of TABQ‐based building bloks, then delves into their significance in battery technology, and finally discusses the potential challenges of TABQ‐based materials in rechargeable batteries.
item.content:encoded:
The tetraamino-benzoquinone (TABQ) unit is a promising building block to construct high-performance electrode materials for rechargeable batteries due to its cost-effectiveness, eco-friendliness, and functional versatility.


Quinone compounds have been widely demonstrated as highly promising electrode materials for rechargeable batteries due to their cost-effectiveness, eco-friendliness, and multifunctionality. Among them, tetraamino-benzoquinone (TABQ) has garnered significant attention as a common building block owing to its potential for large-scale production and unique molecular design flexibility. The amino (–NH2) group in TABQ not only confers its multifunctionality in batteries but also enables the construction of one- to three-dimensional electrode materials. These structural advantages give TABQ-based electrode materials significant application potential in various rechargeable batteries. This minireview first outlines the design strategies and operating principles of TABQ-based building bloks, then delves into their significance in battery technology, and finally discusses the potential challenges of TABQ-based materials in rechargeable batteries.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Earth‐Abundant Metal Catalysis for Alkyl Heck‐Type Reactions With Unbiased Electrophiles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review highlights the concepts and emerging directions of earthabundant‐metal‐catalyzed alkyl Heck‐type reactions involving unbiased alkyl electrophiles, with emphasis on nickel, cobalt, iron, copper, manganese, and bismuth catalysis. It traces their development from early studies in 1968 to advances through 2025, and critically discusses reaction mechanisms and synthetic applications, while outlining current challenges and future opportunities for the sustainable construction of alkene products.
The Mizoroki–Heck reaction is a powerful method for constructing C(sp2)–C(sp2) bonds through the coupling of (pseudo)halide electrophiles with alkenes. In contrast, the alkyl Heck variant, particularly reactions involving unbiased alkyl electrophiles, remains comparatively underdeveloped. Most existing methods rely on palladium catalysts, although recent years have seen growing progress with earth‐abundant‐metals such as nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co), iron (Fe), and copper (Cu). This short review summarizes recent advances in alkyl Heck‐type reactions that couple unbiased alkyl electrophiles with either activated (Type III) or unactivated alkenes (Type IV) under catalysis by earth‐abundant‐metals. The review is organized according to the metal catalyst employed and also includes recent developments in alkyl Heck‐type reactions catalyzed by a main‐group metal, bismuth.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights the concepts and emerging directions of earthabundant-metal-catalyzed alkyl Heck-type reactions involving unbiased alkyl electrophiles, with emphasis on nickel, cobalt, iron, copper, manganese, and bismuth catalysis. It traces their development from early studies in 1968 to advances through 2025, and critically discusses reaction mechanisms and synthetic applications, while outlining current challenges and future opportunities for the sustainable construction of alkene products.


The Mizoroki–Heck reaction is a powerful method for constructing C(sp2)–C(sp2) bonds through the coupling of (pseudo)halide electrophiles with alkenes. In contrast, the alkyl Heck variant, particularly reactions involving unbiased alkyl electrophiles, remains comparatively underdeveloped. Most existing methods rely on palladium catalysts, although recent years have seen growing progress with earth-abundant-metals such as nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co), iron (Fe), and copper (Cu). This short review summarizes recent advances in alkyl Heck-type reactions that couple unbiased alkyl electrophiles with either activated (Type III) or unactivated alkenes (Type IV) under catalysis by earth-abundant-metals. The review is organized according to the metal catalyst employed and also includes recent developments in alkyl Heck-type reactions catalyzed by a main-group metal, bismuth.
item.dc:creator: Xin Chen, Xianglu Peng, Gang‐Wei Wang
item.dc:title: Earth‐Abundant Metal Catalysis for Alkyl Heck‐Type Reactions With Unbiased Electrophiles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Silyl Rearrangements in Gold‐Catalyzed Transformations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 15, 22 April 2026.
item.dc:description:
The synergy between the exceptional ability of gold catalysts to activate unsaturated substrates and the inherent tendency of organosilicon reagents to undergo silyl‐group rearrangements has unlocked a range of novel transformations, enabling access to otherwise challenging molecular architectures. In this review, we highlight recent developments in this rapidly growing field, which has experienced remarkable growth over the past two decades.
By leveraging the impressive ability of gold catalysts to activate unsaturated systems and the natural tendency of organosilicon reagents to undergo silyl group rearrangements, this work has led to the discovery of a variety of new transformations. These developments provide effective methods for building molecular structures that are otherwise difficult to access. In this review, we highlight recent advances in gold‐catalyzed reactions of unsaturated organosilicon reagents, with a particular focus on silyl migration mechanisms. Over the past twenty years, this rapidly growing field has expanded greatly, opening new avenues for synthetic design and applications.
item.content:encoded:
The synergy between the exceptional ability of gold catalysts to activate unsaturated substrates and the inherent tendency of organosilicon reagents to undergo silyl-group rearrangements has unlocked a range of novel transformations, enabling access to otherwise challenging molecular architectures. In this review, we highlight recent developments in this rapidly growing field, which has experienced remarkable growth over the past two decades.


By leveraging the impressive ability of gold catalysts to activate unsaturated systems and the natural tendency of organosilicon reagents to undergo silyl group rearrangements, this work has led to the discovery of a variety of new transformations. These developments provide effective methods for building molecular structures that are otherwise difficult to access. In this review, we highlight recent advances in gold-catalyzed reactions of unsaturated organosilicon reagents, with a particular focus on silyl migration mechanisms. Over the past twenty years, this rapidly growing field has expanded greatly, opening new avenues for synthetic design and applications.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Annulation of Chromene‐2‐Carboxamides With Maleimides via CH Activation: Synthesis of Functionalized Spiro Bis‐γ‐Lactams
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a robust asymmetric tandem [4 + 1] annulation to synthesize functionalized spiro bis‐γ‐lactams. Ring‐opening of the chromone moiety in the products yields multisubstituted spiro bis‐γ‐lactams, enabling efficient construction of a diverse library of such biologically potential derivatives and facilitating relevant synthetic research.
We report an efficient tandem [4 + 1] annulation method for synthesizing functionalized spiro bis‐γ‐lactams from chromone‐2‐carboxamides and maleimides. These spiro compounds integrate chromone, pyrrolidone, and unique spirocyclic skeletons in one molecular framework, endowing them with diverse structural characteristics. Moreover, the chromone ring‐opening reaction of these products provides access to another class of spiro bis‐γ‐lactams bearing multiple substituents. This approach enables the efficient construction of a diverse library of potentially bioactive spiro bis‐γ‐lactam derivatives and serves as a valuable platform for related synthetic studies.
item.content:encoded:
We report a robust asymmetric tandem [4 + 1] annulation to synthesize functionalized spiro bis-γ-lactams. Ring-opening of the chromone moiety in the products yields multisubstituted spiro bis-γ-lactams, enabling efficient construction of a diverse library of such biologically potential derivatives and facilitating relevant synthetic research.


We report an efficient tandem [4 + 1] annulation method for synthesizing functionalized spiro bis-γ-lactams from chromone-2-carboxamides and maleimides. These spiro compounds integrate chromone, pyrrolidone, and unique spirocyclic skeletons in one molecular framework, endowing them with diverse structural characteristics. Moreover, the chromone ring-opening reaction of these products provides access to another class of spiro bis-γ-lactams bearing multiple substituents. This approach enables the efficient construction of a diverse library of potentially bioactive spiro bis-γ-lactam derivatives and serves as a valuable platform for related synthetic studies.
item.dc:creator: Si‐Yi Chen, Qing‐Sheng Zhao, Wei Peng, Cong‐Hai Zhang, Sheng‐Jiao Yan
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Annulation of Chromene‐2‐Carboxamides With Maleimides via CH Activation: Synthesis of Functionalized Spiro Bis‐γ‐Lactams

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Intramolecular NN Bond Formations to Access N‐Heterocycles Under Transition Metal‐Free Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Transition metal–free intramolecular N–N bond formation is a promising route to access N‐heterocycles. This review highlights advances from 2015 to 2025 across electrochemical, photochemical, I2‐promoted, hypervalent iodine‐mediated, and N‐halosuccinimide platforms, discussing reaction conditions, scope, limitations, mechanistic studies, and late‐stage applications. Emerging design principles and unresolved challenges are highlighted to spur new NN‐forming strategies.
Discovery of versatile approaches for the construction of high‐valued N‐rich heterocycles has been a very important research topic in organic chemistry for several years. Owing to their widespread applications in natural products, pharmaceuticals, dye industry, and materials, a multitude of efficient methods to access N‐heterocyclic scaffolds have been uncovered. Of them, transition metal‐free intramolecular NN bond‐forming strategies are distinctive category of transformations and highly preferred because of their wider scope and versatility. In this review, we summarized the existing literature to access N‐heterocycles involving transition metal‐free intramolecular NN bond‐forming approaches from 2015 to 2025. Methodological advancements reported under (1) electrochemical, (2) photochemical, (3) I2‐promoted, (4) hypervalent iodine‐mediated, and (5) N‐halo succinimide‐based conditions are explored. Reaction conditions, representative examples, limitations, post‐synthetic applications, and mechanism are discussed wherever applicable. We expect that the synthetic approaches discussed herein will serve as a comprehensive reference tool and enable further expansion in this field.
item.content:encoded:
Transition metal–free intramolecular N–N bond formation is a promising route to access N-heterocycles. This review highlights advances from 2015 to 2025 across electrochemical, photochemical, I2-promoted, hypervalent iodine-mediated, and N-halosuccinimide platforms, discussing reaction conditions, scope, limitations, mechanistic studies, and late-stage applications. Emerging design principles and unresolved challenges are highlighted to spur new NN-forming strategies.

Owing to their widespread applications in natural products, pharmaceuticals, dye industry, and materials, a multitude of efficient methods to access N-heterocyclic scaffolds have been uncovered. Of them, transition metal-free intramolecular NN bond-forming strategies are distinctive category of transformations and highly preferred because of their wider scope and versatility. In this review, we summarized the existing literature to access N-heterocycles involving transition metal-free intramolecular NN bond-forming approaches from 2015 to 2025. Methodological advancements reported under (1) electrochemical, (2) photochemical, (3) I2-promoted, (4) hypervalent iodine-mediated, and (5) N-halo succinimide-based conditions are explored. Reaction conditions, representative examples, limitations, post-synthetic applications, and mechanism are discussed wherever applicable. We expect that the synthetic approaches discussed herein will serve as a comprehensive reference tool and enable further expansion in this field.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Perimidine Directed C−H Activation/Annulation for the Synthesis of Hexacyclic Cationic Azaperylenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Perimidine has been used as directing group to induce Rh(III)‐catalyzed C−H activation of 2‐aryl‐1H‐perimidines and [4 + 2] annulation with alkynes, leading to hexacyclic cationic azaperylenes A in good to high yields with broad scope. They could also be easily afforded by three‐component triple C−H annulation from all commercial raw materials (naphthalene‐1,8‐diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes). These cationic PAHs show tunable (green to red) luminescence with general > 540 nm emissions.
Annulative π‐extension for the synthesis of cationic azapyrenes has been widely researched, while access to larger π‐systems such as azaperylenes is rare. Herein, perimidine is reported as a fresh directing group to trigger Rh(III)‐catalyzed dual C−H activation/annulation of 2‐aryl perimidines and alkynes, constructing hexacyclic perimidinium salts (benzo‐azaperylenes) A. Salts A could also be synthesized via three‐component triple C−H annulation from commercial naphthalene‐1,8‐diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes. A hexacyclic BF2 complex B1 is conveniently prepared from HO‐substituted perimidine. Three rhodacycles have been detected by ESI‐MS to support the proposed mechanism. The photophysical properties, such as absorptions, emissions, and cyclic voltammetry of these novel cations, are characterized. Salts A show tunable luminescence (516–602 nm) affected by the group from perimidine more than alkyne, and BF2 complex B1 shows emission peaking at 602 nm. The counteranions also have influence on the luminescence, with an order of Cl > BF4 > OTf > SbF6.
item.content:encoded:
Perimidine has been used as directing group to induce Rh(III)-catalyzed C−H activation of 2-aryl-1H-perimidines and [4 + 2] annulation with alkynes, leading to hexacyclic cationic azaperylenes A in good to high yields with broad scope. They could also be easily afforded by three-component triple C−H annulation from all commercial raw materials (naphthalene-1,8-diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes). These cationic PAHs show tunable (green to red) luminescence with general > 540 nm emissions.


Annulative π-extension for the synthesis of cationic azapyrenes has been widely researched, while access to larger π-systems such as azaperylenes is rare. Herein, perimidine is reported as a fresh directing group to trigger Rh(III)-catalyzed dual C−H activation/annulation of 2-aryl perimidines and alkynes, constructing hexacyclic perimidinium salts (benzo-azaperylenes) A. Salts A could also be synthesized via three-component triple C−H annulation from commercial naphthalene-1,8-diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes. A hexacyclic BF2 complex B1 is conveniently prepared from HO-substituted perimidine. Three rhodacycles have been detected by ESI-MS to support the proposed mechanism. The photophysical properties, such as absorptions, emissions, and cyclic voltammetry of these novel cations, are characterized. Salts A show tunable luminescence (516–602 nm) affected by the group from perimidine more than alkyne, and BF2 complex B1 shows emission peaking at 602 nm. The counteranions also have influence on the luminescence, with an order of Cl > BF4 > OTf > SbF6.
item.dc:creator: Hui Li, Haidan Gao, Mingming Yu, Dan Yang, Shiqing Li, Jinhua Wang
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Perimidine Directed C−H Activation/Annulation for the Synthesis of Hexacyclic Cationic Azaperylenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed Alkoxy Radical‐Mediated Ring‐Expansion/C(sp3)–N Coupling of Hemiketal Peroxides with N‐Nucleophiles: A Route to Functionalized Macrolactones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed alkoxy radical‐mediated ring‐expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of fused hemiketal peroxides with various N‐nucleophiles is developed. This method provides efficient access to functionalized medium‐sized and macrolactones bearing an amino fragment at a defined position. The reaction proceeds under mild, redox‐neutral conditions with broad substrate scope, and the leaving group of the peroxide is crucial for the radical initiation step.
A copper‐catalyzed alkoxy radical‐mediated ring‐expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of hemiketal peroxides with N‐nucleophiles has been developed. This protocol allows the construction of functionalized medium‐sized and macrolactones under mild redox‐neutral conditions, exhibiting broad substrate scope and good functional group tolerance. The ring‐expansion amination proceeds efficiently with diverse nitrogen nucleophiles, including amides, sulfamides, indoles, indazoles, and pyrazoles, providing a concise and step‐economical strategy for the synthesis of structurally diverse amino‐substituted lactones. Control studies reveal that the nature of the leaving group in hemiketal peroxides is crucial for the success of this reaction.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-catalyzed alkoxy radical-mediated ring-expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of fused hemiketal peroxides with various N-nucleophiles is developed. This method provides efficient access to functionalized medium-sized and macrolactones bearing an amino fragment at a defined position. The reaction proceeds under mild, redox-neutral conditions with broad substrate scope, and the leaving group of the peroxide is crucial for the radical initiation step.


A copper-catalyzed alkoxy radical-mediated ring-expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of hemiketal peroxides with N-nucleophiles has been developed. This protocol allows the construction of functionalized medium-sized and macrolactones under mild redox-neutral conditions, exhibiting broad substrate scope and good functional group tolerance. The ring-expansion amination proceeds efficiently with diverse nitrogen nucleophiles, including amides, sulfamides, indoles, indazoles, and pyrazoles, providing a concise and step-economical strategy for the synthesis of structurally diverse amino-substituted lactones. Control studies reveal that the nature of the leaving group in hemiketal peroxides is crucial for the success of this reaction.
item.dc:creator: Wenke Li, Hong‐Fei Liu, Xin‐Hua Duan, Li‐Na Guo
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed Alkoxy Radical‐Mediated Ring‐Expansion/C(sp3)–N Coupling of Hemiketal Peroxides with N‐Nucleophiles: A Route to Functionalized Macrolactones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic and Palladium‐Catalyzed Synthesis of Thioglycoside‐1,2,4‐Triazole Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A dual photochemical and Pd‐catalyzed approach allows the selective di‐thioglyco‐conjugation of 1,2,4‐triazoles with thiosugars. This method exploits complementary reactivity pathways, providing access to new thioglycosides scaffolds under mild conditions.
This study presents an efficient approach for the di‐functionalization of 1,2,4‐triazoles with thiosugars, combining photochemical processes and metal‐catalyzed coupling reactions under mild conditions. Exploiting the dual reactivity of the selected 1,2,4‐triazoles, this strategy enables selective and versatile transformations, affording a new family of 1,2,4‐triazole derivatives conjugated with thiosugars with potential for further exploration in biologically relevant studies.
item.content:encoded:
A dual photochemical and Pd-catalyzed approach allows the selective di-thioglyco-conjugation of 1,2,4-triazoles with thiosugars. This method exploits complementary reactivity pathways, providing access to new thioglycosides scaffolds under mild conditions.


This study presents an efficient approach for the di-functionalization of 1,2,4-triazoles with thiosugars, combining photochemical processes and metal-catalyzed coupling reactions under mild conditions. Exploiting the dual reactivity of the selected 1,2,4-triazoles, this strategy enables selective and versatile transformations, affording a new family of 1,2,4-triazole derivatives conjugated with thiosugars with potential for further exploration in biologically relevant studies.
item.dc:creator: Oumayma Ben Jaballah, Franck Le Bideau, Mohamed Lotfi Efrit, Samir Messaoudi
item.dc:title: Photocatalytic and Palladium‐Catalyzed Synthesis of Thioglycoside‐1,2,4‐Triazole Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Diastereoselective Synthesis of Chiral Nitrogen Heterocycles via Reactions of N‐(tert‐Butylsulfinyl) Polyfluoroalkyl Aldimines With Heteroatom‐Substituted Dienes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A catalyst‐free, stereocontrolled addition of Rawal's diene to enantiopure fluorinated aldimines affords diastereomerically pure enaminones. The latter are easily converted into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones and pyrazoles, bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino group. A diastereoselective hetero‐Diels–Alder reaction of Ellman`s fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords N‐sulfinyl‐protected 6‐trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridones.
A stereocontrolled hetero‐Diels–Alder reaction of enantiomeric Ellman's fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords diastereomerically pure N‐sulfinyl‐protected 6‐trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridone. In addition, a highly diastereoselective, catalyst‐free aza‐Mukaiyama addition of Rawal's diene to the CN bond of enantiomeric fluorine‐containing aldimines, RFCHNS(O)tert‐Bu (RF = CF3, CHF2, CF2Me, CF2Ph), has been developed, providing diastereomerically pure enaminones. These chemically and configurationally stable enaminones are cleanly converted under acidic conditions into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino fragment at the 6‐position. Furthermore, cyclocondensation of the enaminones with hydrazines affords the corresponding pyrazoles incorporating a stereochemically pure fluoroalkylated amino group.
item.content:encoded:
A catalyst-free, stereocontrolled addition of Rawal's diene to enantiopure fluorinated aldimines affords diastereomerically pure enaminones. The latter are easily converted into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones and pyrazoles, bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino group. A diastereoselective hetero-Diels–Alder reaction of Ellman`s fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords N-sulfinyl-protected 6-trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridones.


A stereocontrolled hetero-Diels–Alder reaction of enantiomeric Ellman's fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords diastereomerically pure N-sulfinyl-protected 6-trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridone. In addition, a highly diastereoselective, catalyst-free aza-Mukaiyama addition of Rawal's diene to the CN bond of enantiomeric fluorine-containing aldimines, RFCHNS(O)tert-Bu (RF = CF3, CHF2, CF2Me, CF2Ph), has been developed, providing diastereomerically pure enaminones. These chemically and configurationally stable enaminones are cleanly converted under acidic conditions into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino fragment at the 6-position. Furthermore, cyclocondensation of the enaminones with hydrazines affords the corresponding pyrazoles incorporating a stereochemically pure fluoroalkylated amino group.
item.dc:creator: Yuliya V. Rassukana, Roman M. Zakharko, Lyudmyla V. Bezghubenko, Alona S. Cherednichenko, Svitlana V. Shishkina, Petro P. Onys'ko
item.dc:title: Highly Diastereoselective Synthesis of Chiral Nitrogen Heterocycles via Reactions of N‐(tert‐Butylsulfinyl) Polyfluoroalkyl Aldimines With Heteroatom‐Substituted Dienes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Review of Nitration of Anilines: Toward More Sustainable Approaches
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Nitroanilines are important intermediates that can be synthesized through aromatic nitration of anilines. To address the drawbacks of the established mixed acids nitration, various strategies have been developed offering unique selectivity and functional tolerance. A particular emphasis is given to the development of more sustainable pathways using enabling technologies while unveiling their area of improvement to supplant the mixed acids paradigm.
Nitroanilines are versatile building blocks for the synthesis of miscellaneous compounds such as dyes, pharmaceuticals, perfumes and many other targets. To answer the chemical limitations of the well‐developed “mixed acids” conditions (such as harsh conditions, poor regioselectivity, and poor functional group tolerance), several alternatives nitration conditions have been developed specifically for aniline derivatives. In this review, we offer an overview of the miscellaneous existing methodologies to provide nitroanilines through aromatic nitration of pre‐functionalized anilines. The development of more sustainable approaches using enabling technologies is also presented and their sustainability is discussed through a comparative study of E‐factors.
item.content:encoded:
Nitroanilines are important intermediates that can be synthesized through aromatic nitration of anilines. To address the drawbacks of the established mixed acids nitration, various strategies have been developed offering unique selectivity and functional tolerance. A particular emphasis is given to the development of more sustainable pathways using enabling technologies while unveiling their area of improvement to supplant the mixed acids paradigm.


Nitroanilines are versatile building blocks for the synthesis of miscellaneous compounds such as dyes, pharmaceuticals, perfumes and many other targets. To answer the chemical limitations of the well-developed “mixed acids” conditions (such as harsh conditions, poor regioselectivity, and poor functional group tolerance), several alternatives nitration conditions have been developed specifically for aniline derivatives. In this review, we offer an overview of the miscellaneous existing methodologies to provide nitroanilines through aromatic nitration of pre-functionalized anilines. The development of more sustainable approaches using enabling technologies is also presented and their sustainability is discussed through a comparative study of E-factors.
item.dc:creator: Pierre Zimberlin, Zacharias Amara
item.dc:title: A Review of Nitration of Anilines: Toward More Sustainable Approaches

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photochemical Decatungstate‐Catalyzed Hydroacylation of Maleimides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A photocatalytic protocol to access substituted succinimides via tetra‐n‐butylammonium decatungstate photocatalysis is reported. This direct radical‐driven hydroacylation of a variety of N‐substituted maleimides shows tolerance to various aldehydes (or alkanes), affording differentially substituted succinimides in high yields.
Succinimides constitute a useful and coveted structural scaffold in organic synthesis, due to their wide range of applications. Herein, we present an efficient approach for the synthesis of substituted succinimides by means of photocatalysis. Namely, tetra n‐butylammonium decatungstate, a well‐known photocatalyst, was employed under light irradiation, in order to synthesize a variety of succinimide ketones via a direct hydroacylation reaction of N‐substituted maleimides. A diverse range of aldehydes and N‐substituted maleimides show great compatibility with this protocol, providing functionalized succinimides in satisfactory yields. The protocol is applicable to flow conditions, as well as in a gram scale reaction.
item.content:encoded:
A photocatalytic protocol to access substituted succinimides via tetra-n-butylammonium decatungstate photocatalysis is reported. This direct radical-driven hydroacylation of a variety of N-substituted maleimides shows tolerance to various aldehydes (or alkanes), affording differentially substituted succinimides in high yields.


Succinimides constitute a useful and coveted structural scaffold in organic synthesis, due to their wide range of applications. Herein, we present an efficient approach for the synthesis of substituted succinimides by means of photocatalysis. Namely, tetra n-butylammonium decatungstate, a well-known photocatalyst, was employed under light irradiation, in order to synthesize a variety of succinimide ketones via a direct hydroacylation reaction of N-substituted maleimides. A diverse range of aldehydes and N-substituted maleimides show great compatibility with this protocol, providing functionalized succinimides in satisfactory yields. The protocol is applicable to flow conditions, as well as in a gram scale reaction.
item.dc:creator: Manos V. G. Lantzanakis, Naya A. Stini, Christoforos G. Kokotos
item.dc:title: Photochemical Decatungstate‐Catalyzed Hydroacylation of Maleimides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed, Strain‐Promoted Synthesis of Imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from Cyclic Diaryliodonium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A highly efficient Cu‐catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodoniums. This protocol enables the late‐stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules.
A highly efficient copper‐catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridinium derivatives from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodonium salts. Notably, this protocol is suitable for the late‐stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules. Mechanistic studies indicate that the reaction proceeds via a tandem strain‐promoted ring‐opening/intramolecular cyclization sequence.
item.content:encoded:

This protocol enables the late-stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules.


A highly efficient copper-catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2-f]phenanthridinium derivatives from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodonium salts. Notably, this protocol is suitable for the late-stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules. Mechanistic studies indicate that the reaction proceeds via a tandem strain-promoted ring-opening/intramolecular cyclization sequence.

item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed, Strain‐Promoted Synthesis of Imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from Cyclic Diaryliodonium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Chain Elongation Reaction of Tetrahydrofuran for the Synthesis of Cinnamate Esters
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Visible‐light‐induced three‐component reactions of cinnamic acid, diazoesters, and tetrahydrofuran/acetonitrile have been developed for the synthesis of cinnamic acid esters. The synthetic methods, which are performed under mild conditions, feature broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance.
A visible‐light‐induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester is developed. In the presence of THF, this reaction provided chain elongated cinnamic acid esters via formation and ring opening of oxonium ylide with cinnamic acid. The same light‐induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester in MeCN provided simple esters of cinnamic acid.
item.content:encoded:
Visible-light-induced three-component reactions of cinnamic acid, diazoesters, and tetrahydrofuran/acetonitrile have been developed for the synthesis of cinnamic acid esters. The synthetic methods, which are performed under mild conditions, feature broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance.


A visible-light-induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester is developed. In the presence of THF, this reaction provided chain elongated cinnamic acid esters via formation and ring opening of oxonium ylide with cinnamic acid. The same light-induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester in MeCN provided simple esters of cinnamic acid.

item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Chain Elongation Reaction of Tetrahydrofuran for the Synthesis of Cinnamate Esters

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances of Photo/Electro‐Enzymatic Catalytic Systems in Organic Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in integrating photochemistry, electrochemistry, and enzyme catalysis for organic synthesis, focusing on three multicatalytic systems: photo‐enzymatic, electro‐enzymatic, and photo‐electro‐enzymatic catalysis. Their mechanisms, advantages, and challenges are discussed, along with future directions in catalyst design, system optimization, mechanistic studies, and industrial applications for green synthesis.
In recent years, the integration of photochemistry, electrochemistry, and enzyme catalysis has brought revolutionary advances to the field of organic synthesis. This multicatalytic synergistic strategy not only significantly enhances reaction efficiency and selectivity but also demonstrates great potential in green synthesis, chiral construction, carbon–carbon/carbon–heteroatom bond formation, and the synthesis of complex molecules. This review systematically summarizes the applications of three multicatalytic systems—photo‐enzymatic catalysis, electro‐enzymatic catalysis, and photo‐electro‐enzymatic catalysis—in organic synthesis, and discusses the mechanistic features, advantages, and challenges of each system. Finally, the future development directions in the field are outlined, including catalyst design, system optimization, mechanistic studies, and industrial applications, to further advance this green synthetic strategy.
item.content:encoded:

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Julolidine‐Functionalized Amphiphilic Aza‐BODIPY Dyes: Synthesis, Near‐Infraed‐II Emission, and pH‐Response
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Four 1,7‐julolidine‐substituted amphiphilic aza‐BODIPY dyes 1a‐d bearing hydrophilic side chains at the 3,5‐phenyls were synthesized. Modulating the number, position, and structure of electron‐donating linkages (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) on the 3,5‐phenyl rings of these dyes enables tunable optical properties with NIR II fluorescence with up to 1400 nm (dye 1b in DMSO, emission maximum = 1109 nm).
A series of amphiphilic aza‐BODIPY dyes (1a‐d) bearing two julolidine units at 1,7‐positions of the azadipyrromethene core and hydrophilic oligo(ethylene glycol) chains at the 3,5‐phenyls were synthesized and characterized. All dyes feature broad, panchromatic absorption bands from 350 nm up to 1000–1100 nm in CH2Cl2 and exhibit near‐infrared (NIR‐II) emission bands up to 1400 nm. Solvent‐dependent absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic studies reveal pronounced positive solvatochromism of these dyes and further Lippert–Mataga analyses reveal their efficient intramolecular charge transfer character. The optical properties of these dyes are strongly influenced by the substitution patterns as well as the linker structures (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) for the hydrophilic moieties at the 3,5‐phenyls, which is further elucidated by density functional theory calculations. Notably, dye 1b exhibits the most red‐shifted emission (1109 nm in DMSO) with a high fluorescence quantum yield (2.0%). In addition, all dyes display reversible spectroscopic pH‐response. These results underscore the promising application potential of these new aza‐BODIPY dyes.
item.content:encoded:
Four 1,7-julolidine-substituted amphiphilic aza-BODIPY dyes 1a-d bearing hydrophilic side chains at the 3,5-phenyls were synthesized. Modulating the number, position, and structure of electron-donating linkages (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) on the 3,5-phenyl rings of these dyes enables tunable optical properties with NIR II fluorescence with up to 1400 nm (dye 1b in DMSO, emission maximum = 1109 nm).


A series of amphiphilic aza-BODIPY dyes (1a-d) bearing two julolidine units at 1,7-positions of the azadipyrromethene core and hydrophilic oligo(ethylene glycol) chains at the 3,5-phenyls were synthesized and characterized. All dyes feature broad, panchromatic absorption bands from 350 nm up to 1000–1100 nm in CH2Cl2 and exhibit near-infrared (NIR-II) emission bands up to 1400 nm. Solvent-dependent absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic studies reveal pronounced positive solvatochromism of these dyes and further Lippert–Mataga analyses reveal their efficient intramolecular charge transfer character. The optical properties of these dyes are strongly influenced by the substitution patterns as well as the linker structures (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) for the hydrophilic moieties at the 3,5-phenyls, which is further elucidated by density functional theory calculations. Notably, dye 1b exhibits the most red-shifted emission (1109 nm in DMSO) with a high fluorescence quantum yield (2.0%). In addition, all dyes display reversible spectroscopic pH-response. These results underscore the promising application potential of these new aza-BODIPY dyes.
item.dc:creator: Mengyu Hao, Hongfei Pan, Xuefeng Kuang, Hanjun Huang, Xiang‐Kui Ren, Zhijian Chen
item.dc:title: Julolidine‐Functionalized Amphiphilic Aza‐BODIPY Dyes: Synthesis, Near‐Infraed‐II Emission, and pH‐Response

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Selective Synthesis of Spirocyclobutanes via [2 + 2] Cycloaddition of α,β‐Unsaturated Acenaphthenone and Acenaphthylene
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A visible‐light‐driven [2 + 2] photocycloaddition enables the efficient construction of spirocyclobutane scaffolds from α,β‐unsaturated acenaphthylenones and acenaphthylene under mild conditions. High diastereoselectivity (up to dr > 99:1) is achieved. Product selectivity is governed by substrate structure, and the products are readily isolated by simple filtration without chromatography.
The cyclobutane framework serves as an important class of rigid scaffolds with broad applications in medicinal chemistry and advanced materials. In particular, spirocyclobutanes have emerged as a key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules owing to their distinctive three‐dimensional geometry and enhanced metabolic stability. Herein, we report an efficient and practical synthetic strategy that constructs the spirocyclobutane core in a single step via a visible‐light‐promoted, copper sulfate‐catalyzed intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition between α,β‐unsaturated acenaphthenones and acenaphthylene. This operationally simple protocol requires only straightforward filtration to afford products in high purity. Meanwhile, the good functional group tolerance and broad substrate scope were well demonstrated by delivering the diverse target spirocyclobutanes in moderate to excellent yields (50%–92%) with high stereoselectivity. This novel method thus offers a practical, economical, and sustainable alternative for the construction of spirocyclobutane derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
A visible-light-driven [2 + 2] photocycloaddition enables the efficient construction of spirocyclobutane scaffolds from α,β-unsaturated acenaphthylenones and acenaphthylene under mild conditions. High diastereoselectivity (up to dr > 99:1) is achieved. Product selectivity is governed by substrate structure, and the products are readily isolated by simple filtration without chromatography.


The cyclobutane framework serves as an important class of rigid scaffolds with broad applications in medicinal chemistry and advanced materials. In particular, spirocyclobutanes have emerged as a key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules owing to their distinctive three-dimensional geometry and enhanced metabolic stability. Herein, we report an efficient and practical synthetic strategy that constructs the spirocyclobutane core in a single step via a visible-light-promoted, copper sulfate-catalyzed intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition between α,β-unsaturated acenaphthenones and acenaphthylene. This operationally simple protocol requires only straightforward filtration to afford products in high purity. Meanwhile, the good functional group tolerance and broad substrate scope were well demonstrated by delivering the diverse target spirocyclobutanes in moderate to excellent yields (50%–92%) with high stereoselectivity. This novel method thus offers a practical, economical, and sustainable alternative for the construction of spirocyclobutane derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Yuqing Sun, Zhaojuan Lan, Bing Sun, Fang‐Lin Zhang
item.dc:title: Highly Selective Synthesis of Spirocyclobutanes via [2 + 2] Cycloaddition of α,β‐Unsaturated Acenaphthenone and Acenaphthylene

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Versatile Synthesis of Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol and Related Compounds by Protecting‐Group‐Free Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An operationally simple method for synthesizing Δ9‐tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9‐THC) and its analogs via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling provides rapid access to diverse Δ9‐THC analogs, with potential applications for affording authentic standards of suspect compounds in forensic contexts with limited facilities.
Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9‐THC) was synthesized via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling of a triflate precursor, derived from an inexpensive terpenoid and phloroglucinol, with a boronic acid derivative. This method also provides easy access to Δ9‐THC derivatives bearing a variety of n‐alkyl, aryl and alkenyl side chains and is applicable to other cannabinoids, including cannabidiol and Δ8‐THC derivatives, without requiring strictly anhydrous conditions, special equipment, or protection of the phenolic hydroxyl group. It is expected to be useful in preparing authentic standards of suspected illicit drugs in forensic laboratories where expertise and facilities for organic synthesis may be limited.
item.content:encoded:

Δ9-Tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9-THC) was synthesized via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling of a triflate precursor, derived from an inexpensive terpenoid and phloroglucinol, with a boronic acid derivative. This method also provides easy access to Δ9-THC derivatives bearing a variety of n-alkyl, aryl and alkenyl side chains and is applicable to other cannabinoids, including cannabidiol and Δ8-THC derivatives, without requiring strictly anhydrous conditions, special equipment, or protection of the phenolic hydroxyl group. It is expected to be useful in preparing authentic standards of suspected illicit drugs in forensic laboratories where expertise and facilities for organic synthesis may be limited.
